summary:
The Kingdom of Dahomey (c. 1600–1894 AD) was a powerful West African state in the region of modern Benin that rose to prominence in the 17th century and became one of the most formidable military powers in West Africa — renowned for its disciplined army, sophisticated bureaucracy, and notably its corps of female soldiers (the Agojie, called 'Dahomey Amazons' by Europeans) who formed a significant portion of the royal army and were feared throughout the region. Dahomey was founded in the Abomey plateau by the Fon people, expanding to conquer neighboring kingdoms including Allada (1724) and the coastal trading port of Ouidah (1727), which gave it direct access to the Atlantic and the European slave trade.

Dahomey's relationship with the [[event:2341|Atlantic slave trade]] was central to its economy and power — the kingdom raided neighboring peoples extensively, capturing enslaved people who were sold to European traders through Ouidah. This made Dahomey a major supplier of enslaved Africans to the Americas while simultaneously making it dependent on European trade goods, particularly firearms that maintained its military advantage over neighbors. The kingdom was known for annual 'Customs' ceremonies involving human sacrifice of war captives — practices that shocked European visitors and provided fodder for anti-African racism while reflecting a complex ritual and political system. Dahomey successfully resisted [[event:2453|Oyo Empire]] domination through most of the 18th century; its conquest by France in 1894 ended over two centuries of independence and made it the core of French Dahomey, later Benin.
